pages.title: Introduction to Hooks
pages.content:
Hooks are a new addition in React 16.8. They let you use state and other React features without writing a class.

### Why Hooks?
- **Reusability**: Share stateful logic between components easily.
- **Complexity**: Avoid the confusion of lifecycle methods like `componentDidMount`.
- **Simplicity**: Functional components are easier to read and test.

pages.title: useState: Managing State
pages.content:
The `useState` hook is the most basic hook. It returns a stateful value and a function to update it.

```tsx
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
```

### Best Practices:
- Use multiple state variables for different pieces of data.
- Don't put everything in one big object unless they always change together.

pages.title: useEffect: Side Effects
pages.content:
The `useEffect` hook lets you perform side effects in function components. It's a combination of `componentDidMount`, `componentDidUpdate`, and `componentWillUnmount`.

### Dependency Array:
- `[]`: Runs only once on mount.
- `[prop]`: Runs on mount and whenever `prop` changes.
- No array: Runs on every render.

pages.title: useContext: Global State
pages.content:
Context provides a way to pass data through the component tree without having to pass props down manually at every level.

### Usage:
1. Create a context: `const MyContext = createContext(defaultValue);`
2. Provide context: `<MyContext.Provider value={...}>`
3. Consume context: `const value = useContext(MyContext);`

pages.title: useReducer: Complex State
pages.content: For complex state logic that involves multiple sub-values or when the next state depends on the previous one, `useReducer` is usually preferable to `useState`.

pages.title: useCallback: Memoizing Functions
pages.content: Returns a memoized version of the callback that only changes if one of the dependencies has changed. Useful when passing callbacks to optimized child components.

pages.title: useMemo: Memoizing Values
pages.content: Returns a memoized value. Pass a 'create' function and an array of dependencies. `useMemo` will only recompute the memoized value when one of the dependencies has changed.

pages.title: useRef: Accessing DOM
pages.content: Returns a mutable ref object whose `.current` property is initialized to the passed argument. The returned object will persist for the full lifetime of the component.

pages.title: Custom Hooks
pages.content: A custom Hook is a JavaScript function whose name starts with ”use” and that may call other Hooks. It allows you to extract component logic into reusable functions.

pages.title: Rules of Hooks
pages.content:
1. Only call Hooks at the top level. Don’t call Hooks inside loops, conditions, or nested functions.
2. Only call Hooks from React function components or custom Hooks.

pages.title: The New Paradigm
pages.content: The App Router is a new way to build Next.js applications using React Server Components. It supports shared layouts, nested routing, loading states, and error handling.

pages.title: File-System Routing
pages.content: Next.js uses a file-system based router where folders are used to define routes. Each folder represents a route segment that maps to a URL segment.

pages.title: Layouts and Templates
pages.content: A layout is UI that is shared between multiple pages. On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not re-render.

pages.title: Server Components
pages.content: By default, Next.js uses Server Components. This allows you to render components on the server and reduce the amount of JavaScript sent to the client.

pages.title: Client Components
pages.content: To use interactivity, browser APIs, or state, you must use Client Components by adding the `'use client'` directive at the top of the file.

pages.title: Data Fetching
pages.content: Next.js extends the native `fetch` API to allow you to configure caching and revalidation for each fetch request on the server.

pages.title: Streaming and Suspense
pages.content: Streaming allows you to break down the page's HTML into smaller chunks and progressively send those chunks from the server to the client.

pages.title: Error Handling
pages.content: The `error.js` file convention allows you to gracefully handle unexpected runtime errors in nested routes.

pages.title: Loading UI
pages.content: The `loading.js` file convention helps you create meaningful Loading UI with React Suspense.

pages.title: Server Actions
pages.content: Server Actions are asynchronous functions that are executed on the server. They can be used in Client and Server Components to handle form submissions and data mutations.

pages.title: Beyond the Basics
pages.content: Advanced TypeScript involves mastering the type system to create safer and more expressive codebases.

pages.title: Generics
pages.content: Generics allow you to create components that work over a variety of types rather than a single one. This allows users to consume these components and use their own types.

pages.title: Conditional Types
pages.content: Conditional types help describe the relation between the types of inputs and outputs. `T extends U ? X : Y`

pages.title: Mapped Types
pages.content: Mapped types allow you to create new types based on existing ones by iterating over keys.

pages.title: Template Literal Types
pages.content: Template literal types build on string literal types, and have the ability to expand into many strings via unions.

pages.title: Utility Types
pages.content: TypeScript provides several utility types to facilitate common type transformations like `Partial`, `Required`, `Readonly`, and `Pick`.

pages.title: Type Guards
pages.content: Type guarding is the process of narrowing down the type of an object within a conditional block.

pages.title: Discriminated Unions
pages.content: A common pattern in functional programming where you use a literal property to distinguish between different types in a union.

pages.title: Declaration Merging
pages.content: Declaration merging is when the compiler merges two separate declarations declared with the same name into a single definition.

pages.title: Advanced Patterns
pages.content: Using mixins, decorators, and advanced inference to build robust libraries and applications.

pages.title: Utility-First Mindset
pages.content: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first CSS framework packed with classes like `flex`, `pt-4`, `text-center` and `rotate-90` that can be composed to build any design, directly in your markup.

pages.title: Configuration
pages.content: The `tailwind.config.js` file is where you define your theme, plugins, and any other customizations.

pages.title: Responsive Design
pages.content: Using responsive modifiers like `sm:`, `md:`, `lg:`, and `xl:` to build adaptive interfaces.

pages.title: Hover, Focus, and Other States
pages.content: Tailwind includes modifiers for almost every pseudo-class, like `hover:`, `focus:`, `active:`, etc.

pages.title: Dark Mode
pages.content: Tailwind includes a `dark` variant that lets you style your site differently when dark mode is enabled.

pages.title: Reusing Styles
pages.content: Using `@apply` to extract repeated utility patterns into custom CSS classes, or better yet, using component abstractions.

pages.title: Customizing the Theme
pages.content: How to extend the default theme with your own colors, spacing, and fonts.

pages.title: Plugins
pages.content: Extending Tailwind with custom plugins to add new utilities, components, or base styles.

pages.title: Optimization
pages.content: Using PurgeCSS (built-in) to remove unused CSS and keep your production bundles tiny.

pages.title: Best Practices
pages.content: Organizing classes, using design tokens, and maintaining a consistent design system.

pages.title: The Art of the Prompt
pages.content: Prompt engineering is the process of structuring text that can be interpreted and understood by a generative AI model.

pages.title: Zero-Shot vs Few-Shot
pages.content: Zero-shot is asking the model a question without examples. Few-shot is providing a few examples to guide the model's response.

pages.title: Chain of Thought
pages.content: Encouraging the model to 'think step by step' to improve reasoning and accuracy for complex tasks.

pages.title: System Instructions
pages.content: Using system-level prompts to define the AI's persona, constraints, and overall behavior.

pages.title: Prompt Templates
pages.content: Building reusable structures for common tasks like summarization, translation, or code generation.

pages.title: Handling Hallucinations
pages.content: Techniques to reduce false information, such as grounding the model in provided text or using self-consistency checks.

pages.title: Iterative Refinement
pages.content: The process of testing and adjusting prompts to achieve the best possible output.

pages.title: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
pages.content: Combining LLMs with external knowledge bases to provide up-to-date and specific information.

pages.title: Advanced Techniques
pages.content: Using delimiters, specifying output formats (JSON/Markdown), and controlling temperature/top-p.

pages.title: Ethical Considerations
pages.content: Avoiding bias, ensuring safety, and understanding the limitations of AI models.
